summary: McKenzie praises e-junkie's customer service after experiencing email delivery issues with his registration codes. He describes a technical problem where customers couldn't receive registration emails, and how he faced the prospect of writing complex Perl code to work around it. After requesting a feature to pass registration codes as URL parameters, e-junkie not only responded quickly but implemented the feature within 24 hours, apologizing for the 'delay.' The post serves as a testimonial for responsive vendor support.
key_points: Email delivery failures for registration codes created a real business problem requiring a technical workaround
The proposed Perl workaround involved race conditions and timing issues that made it fragile and complex
A simple feature request (passing one additional URL parameter) eliminated the need for complex server-side code entirely
e-junkie implemented a custom feature request within 24 hours for a $5/month customer, demonstrating exceptional service
money_quotes: Aside from this involving evil Perl code (is there any other kind?), there were some potentially nasty timing issues involved.
Pop quiz: what happens if the customer arrives before I have the request from e-junkie to generate the registration code?
If they did that, fixing this problem would be a matter of writing ten lines of Javascript.

I definately wasn't expecting the next email to say 'We were going to mail you back when this was implemented, but it turns out our engineering team will require 24 hours to get to it. Sorry for the delay.'
So there you have it: e-junkie, the best $5 per month I ever spent.
key_insight: Exceptional vendor responsiveness — implementing a custom feature request in 24 hours for a $5/month customer — can turn a minor service into a passionate advocate's recommendation.
